coredata.dc:title: The design of aggregation-induced fluorescence sensor based on the cetyltrimethylammonium bromide-mediated nitrogen-doped carbon dots for selective detection of Hg2+
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Yayun
Zou, Xue
Chen, Qiumeng
Tian, Yulu
Gong, Zhengjun
Fan, Meikun
coredata.dc:description: This work reported an economical and sensitive fluorescence sensor for mercury (II) ions based on the cetyltrimethylammonium bromide-mediated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The NCDs, obtained by a solvothermal method, exhibit emission with low quantum yield (6%) that can be quenched by various ions such as Cu2+, Cr6+, Co2+, and Hg2+. After adding cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), the two produced aggregation-induced fluorescence effect, coupled with electrostatic interaction, and the quantum yield increased to 32%. The spatial structure formed by CTAB/NCDs could selectively capture Hg2+ and make interact with the surface groups of NCDs to cause fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration showed a linear response in the range of 0.15–1 mM and 0.16–10.24 μM (R2 = 0.998), and the detection limit was 85.71 nM. The fluorescent sensor was used to determine Hg2+ in environmental waters with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced fluorescence effect
Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide
Carbon dots
Quenching mechanism
Mercury (II) ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of NCDs. Inset: TEM image of one carbon nanoparticle; (b) The size distribution histogram of NCDs; (c) TEM images of CTAB@NCDs. Inset: TEM image of one carbon nanoparticle; (d) The size distribution histogram of CTAB@NCDs; (e) FTIR spectrum of the NCDs, CTAB and CTAB@NCDs; (f) XPS spectra of CTAB@NCDs.

(a) The influence of different surfactants on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs; (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay graphs of NCDs, CTAB@NCDs and CTAB@NCDs/Hg2+; (c) Effect of metal ions on the fluorescence efficiency of NCDs; (d) The influence of metal ions on the fluorescence efficiency of NCDs@CTAB; (e) The UV absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of CTAB@NCDs, the inset was the photo under UV lamp; (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of CTAB@NCDs under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) CTAB@NCDs with continuous fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of Hg2+ and (b) continuous dot plots at 550 nm; (c) Linear calibration chart of CTAB@NCDs for detecting higher concentration of Hg2+; (d) Linear calibration chart of CTAB@NCDs for detecting low concentration Hg2+.
Comparison of Hg2+ detection methods based on nanomaterials in recent years.

head.ce:title: The design of aggregation-induced fluorescence sensor based on the cetyltrimethylammonium bromide-mediated nitrogen-doped carbon dots for selective detection of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu, 611756, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu, 611756, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu, 611756, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State-province Joint Engineering Laboratory of Spatial Information Technology of High-Speed Rail Safety, Chengdu, 611756, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu, 611756, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reported an economical and sensitive fluorescence sensor for mercury (II) ions based on the cetyltrimethylammonium bromide-mediated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The NCDs, obtained by a solvothermal method, exhibit emission with low quantum yield (6%) that can be quenched by various ions such as Cu2+, Cr6+, Co2+, and Hg2+. After adding cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), the two produced aggregation-induced fluorescence effect, coupled with electrostatic interaction, and the quantum yield increased to 32%. The spatial structure formed by CTAB/NCDs could selectively capture Hg2+ and make interact with the surface groups of NCDs to cause fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration showed a linear response in the range of 0.15–1 mM and 0.16–10.24 μM (R2 = 0.998), and the detection limit was 85.71 nM. The fluorescent sensor was used to determine Hg2+ in environmental waters with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: As the most harmful heavy metal, mercury (Hg) has the features of high toxicity and easy accumulation and results in serious harm to the ecological environment and life health [1]. Mercury (II) ions (Hg2+) have the characteristics of strong toxicity, non-degradability, and strong fluidity. Digestive tract injury, nervous system injury, blood disease, and other human injuries or diseases might caused for the accumulation of Hg2+ and even lead to death [2]. Under the action of microorganisms in water, the Hg2+ can transform into methylmercury or other organic mercury ions, which are more toxic than inorganic mercury ions. Moreover, organic mercury ions get easier to accumulate and enrich in organisms due to the fat solubility. Both the USA and the European Union have made regulations on the maximum concentration of Hg2+. So, there has been a growing interest in developing efficient methods for Hg2+ detection. At present, traditional detection methods of Hg2+ include spectrophotometry, electrochemical analysis method [3], atomic absorption spectrometry [4], fluorescence spectrometry [5], and so on. In recent years, fluorescent sensor analytical methods made from nanomaterials has attracted much attention due to their advantages of high speed, high selectivity, and high sensitivity. But, some flaws such as high toxicity, high cost (gold and silver nanoparticles), complex synthesis, and poor biocompatibility (semiconductor nanoprobes) of fluorescence probe have plagued researchers [6,7]. In comparison, carbon dots (CDs) have good biocompatibility and mild toxicity [8], and are used in imaging, medical diagnosis, sensors, and so on [9–11], which have broad research and application prospects. However, CDs still face severe challenges such as low fluorescence quantum yield, poor sensor sensitivity, and selectivity, etc.

To improve the fluorescence characteristics of CDs, elemental doping or functionalization of CDs is a feasible method that has been used more in recent years [12,13]. In the carbon dot, nitrogen is a typical dopant, which has an atomic size equal to carbon. It can act as an n-type impurity and provide excess electrons to move the Fermi level upward, thereby changing the optical properties of the CDs and improving light stability [10]. In addition, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), as a critical positively charged surfactant, was widely used in the field of inorganic material synthesis. Its modified material can also improve the color rendering and sensitivity of the probe and enhance the sensing performance of the material [14,15]. Similarly, the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [16], a phenomenon discovered by Tang and his team also supported the improvement of fluorescence quantum yield. AIE is a phenomenon of enhanced luminescence, some particular molecules hardly emit light in the solution, but the phenomenon of enhanced luminescence in the state of aggregation or in the form of a solid film [17]. The aggregation-enhanced emission (AEE) system derived from the AIE principle has become a feasible way to improve the fluorescence quantum yield. Tang's group used hyperbranched polymers with aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement (AEE) properties as probes. It proved that the addition of picric acid detecting explosives could significantly enhance the fluorescence of polymer nano-aggregates [18]. Therefore, adding the appropriate amount of CTAB in the preparation of the sensor to induce AEE was a feasible approach for further to improve the specificity and sensitivity.

So, we prepared the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) by one-step hydrothermal treatment. After the introduction of CTAB, the NCDs aggregated due to mutual electrostatic interaction. The obtained CTAB@NCDs owned high fluorescence (quantum yield 32%). The space structure of CTAB@NCDs would capture Hg2+ with high selectivity and cause fluorescence quenching. In addition, the fluorescent sensor was successfully applied to environmental water bodies, which was a simple, fast, and environmentally friendly fluorescence detection method.

Citric acid (C₆H₈O₇), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), Tween-80, cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC), and sodium acetate (CH3COONa) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), acetic acid (CH3COOH), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS), mercury Chloride (HgCl2) and sodium lauryl sulfate (SDS) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). The metal ion solutions used were all made from their nitrates, sulfates or chlorides. All of these reagents were of analytical grade, and the solvent for the experiment was ultrapure water.

The fluorescence spectrum was recorded by FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) at room temperature. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was obtained by USB-2000 UV–vis spectrometer (Ocean Optics, USA) in the range of 200–700 nm with a slit of 2 nm. The FT-IR spectrum was obtained in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, USA). The XPS and XRD spectra of NCDs were performed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) and X'Pert PRO X-ray diffraction (XRD) (PANalytical X'Pert PRO, Holland). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by using a JEM-2100F electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The mixing procedure of CTAB solution and NCDs was accomplished by using a vortex mixer (Scilogex, USA).

Similar to the previous reports, NCDs were prepared by solvothermal method with minor modifications [19]. 1 g of citric acid and 1.6 g of urea were dissolved into 20 mL of DMF and then sonicated to form a transparent solution. The above solution was transferred into a 50 mL high-pressure reactor and heated to 180 °C for 8 h in the oven. Carbon dot solutions in red-brown (NCDs) were obtained after being naturally cooled to room temperature. 4 mL NCDs solution and 6 mL of 50 mg/mL NaOH solution were thoroughly mixed, and 1 mL of 20 mg/mL CTAB solution was added to form CTAB@NCDs solution. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min, and then further purified using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 da for 48 h to remove unreacted materials. Finally, solid CTAB@NCDs were obtained by freeze-drying for further characterization.

The quantum yield (QY) was calculated by formula 1 with the determination standard, that used fluorescein (η = 1.33, QY = 95%) in 0.10 mol/L NaOH solution.
(1)
Φ =
Φ R
×
I
I R
×
A
A R
×
η 2
η R 2
where Φ is the QY, A is the optical density, I is the integrated emission intensity, and η is the refractive index. The absorbance of the reference and the NCDs were both kept below 0.05 at λex = 440 nm to minimize re-absorption effects.

The fluorescence quenching efficiency F0/F1-1 was used as the signal for determining Hg2+, where F0 and F1 are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. By screening and optimizing CTAB@NCDs substrate preparation conditions (such as reactant concentration, preparation time, preparation temperature, and surfactant selection), the fluorescence spectra of the system under different excitation wavelengths were recorded. When the excitation wavelength was 440 nm, the best emission fluorescence of CTAB@NCDs at 550 nm was obtained. By optimizing the reaction conditions to get the best experimental conditions. 100 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 3) was mixed with 200 μL of CTAB@NCDs, followed by adding 1600 μL of various concentrations of Hg2+ solution. This mixed solution was incubated in a water bath at room temperature for 5 min and measured the fluorescence spectrum was in the range of 500–700 nm. A variety of metal ions and anions were adopted to evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the CTAB@NCDs. All the experiments were repeated three times.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of NCDs and CTAB@NCDs were shown in Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1c, which showed the morphology of NCDs and CTAB@NCDs were both spherical, and CTAB@NCDs had higher dispersion than NCDs. The particle size distributions of NCDs and CTAB@NCDs were shown in Fig. 1b and d. The average diameters of NCDs and CTAB@NCDs were about 3.25 nm and 4.5 nm, respectively and judged from 100 random particles. The particle size change may be due to the surrounding of long alkyl chain (16-carbon) organic molecules so that NCDs may be arranged or aggregated, and the particle size distribution is more uniform. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images taken from one nanoparticle (inset in Fig. 1a and c) showed the corresponding crystal structures with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphene [20]. Besides, the XRD results of the CTAB@NCDs (Fig. S1) displayed a diffused peak centered at 2θ = 23.4°, which corresponds to the (002) infraction patterns of the graphitic carbon, indicating the existence of amorphous carbon [21,22].

The functional groups on the original CDs and modified CDs were characterized by FI-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1e, the broadband (Tensile vibration) at 3100-3700 cm−1 corresponded to O-H and N-H groups [23]. The firm absorption peaks at 2918 and 2848 cm−1 were related to the symmetric and antisymmetric vibration modes of -CH2 group in CTAB [24]. In contrastly, the stretching vibrations of C=O at 1719 cm−1, C-O-C at 1025 cm−1, and C-N at 1643 cm−1, also COO− at 1580 and 1469 cm−1 were detected in both NCDs and CTAB@NCDS samples, which indicated that CTAB was successfully doped on the carbon dots. The surface composition and elemental analysis of CTAB@NCDs by XPS were shown in Fig. 1f. Three peaks of 284, 400, and 530 eV were found in the XPS spectrum, which was attributed to C1s (80.37%), N1s (7.5%), and O1s (12.13%), respectively. The amount of carbon further confirmed the successful modification of NCDs by CTAB [25]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum is displayed in Fig. S2a. It was noted that the peaks at 283.9, 285.4, 286.8, and 288.8 eV were assigned to C-C, C-N, C-O, and C=N/C=O, respectively [26]. The high-resolution O1s peak (Fig. 2 b) can be fitted to 3 peaks at 530.1, 531.0, and 531.9 eV, corresponding to C-OH, C=O, and C-O-C, respectively [27]. In addition, the N1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) could be synthesized into three peaks at 397.3, 399.2, and 402.1 eV, attributing to C-N bond and C=N bond, respectively, which were in accord with the FT-IR analysis. Furthermore, the SERS spectra of NCDs and corresponding CTAB@NCDs were compared in Fig. S2d. It can be seen that the peak intensities of NCDs containing CTAB at 1350 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 are significantly increased, which were considered to be related to the role of cationic surfactants in adjusting the distance between carbon particles [28,29].

To further reveal the fluorescence properties of the prepared CTAB@NCDs, the effects of the introduction of anionic surfactants (SDS and SDBS), cationic surfactants (CTAB and CTAC), and non-ionic surfactants (PVP and Tween-80) on the fluorescence signal of NCDs under the same conditions were compared. As shown in Fig. 2a, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced by 1.12, 0.80, −0.20, 0.002, −0.76, and −0.24 times when different surfactants were added (1 mM). It could be concluded that cationic surfactant had an obvious fluorescence enhancement effect on NCDs, and CTAB had the best effect. This is due to the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged NCDs and the positively charged CTAB, which effectively reduces the vibration and rotation of the NCDs [30]. Under the action of the long alkyl chain molecules of CTAB, NCDs forms a highly ordered structure, thereby weakening the interaction between it and solvent molecules, resulting in the aggregation-induced fluorescence effect, thereby enhanced fluorescence emission [31,32]. For other cationic surfactants, the research of Ren et al. showed that cationic surfactants (such as benzyldodecyldimethylammonium bromide, dodecylpyridinium chloride, and CTAB) has the effect of enhancing the photoluminescence of carbon dots [32]. Chakrabarty et al. also proved the fluorescence enhancement effect of dodecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, tetradecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, and CTAB, and further showed that the increase in surfactant chain length can increase the density of micellar units, resulting in an increase in the number of probes located in the interface area, thereby increasing the fluorescence intensity [33].

For more detail of this enhancement, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL emission spectrum of CTAB@NCDs are drawn, which are presented in Fig. 2e. The absorption spectrum of the samples represented a narrow peak at 340 nm. This attribute to that the excited hole electron pairs were capture via local surface state, which led to strong FL emission [34]. The broad absorption peak was observed at 440 nm, indicating that the existence of an extended π-π conjugate structure [35]. The fluorescence spectrum under the excitation of 440 nm corresponded to the maximum emission wavelength of 553 nm, and the dispersion exposed to ultraviolet light irradiation appeared yellow-green (the inset in Fig. 2e). The enhanced excitation wavelength increases led to fluorescence emission peak shifting to longer wavelengths over the samples of CTAB@NCDs (Fig. 2f). This is due to the different surface states responsible for emission were excited at one wavelength, resulting in a broad emission spectrum with multiple shoulders [36]. In addition, the steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs in the presence and absence of CTAB, and the determination standard was calculated according to formula 1. The results showed that the QY of NCDs was 6% in the absence lack of CTAB, while the QY increased to 32% after the addition of CTAB. At the same time, the fluorescence attenuation curve is fitted by a double exponential function at the excitation wavelength of λex = 450 nm to measure the attenuation of photoluminescence and calculate the lifetime. The comparison data of double exponential fitting are shown in Table S1. The attenuation times of NCDs and CTAB@NCDs are 3.01 ns and 3.44 ns, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2b. It can be shown that the increase of QY and the extension of fluorescence lifetime are due to the electrostatic interaction between the two stabilizing the excited state of the anion carbon dots, thereby improving the short-lived emission trap states or reducing the competitive nonradiation processes [32,37]. In conclusion, the addition of CTAB could improve the QY and luminescence efficiency of this fluorescence sensor, which is a convenient and efficient post functionalization strategy.

The change of micro-environment of the solution system is accompanied while sensitizing the fluorescence behavior of the carbon dots when the surfactant concentration reaches critical micelle concentration as literature reported previously [31,32]. The different concentrations of CTAB were introduced in NCDs via fluorescence comparison spectrum (Fig. S3a). It can be seen that in the presence of 10 mM critical micelle concentration, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was increased and gradually reached a plateau, which was three times versus unmodified NCDs. To compare its sensitization effects, NCDs and CTAB@NCDs were used separately to detect metal ions. The results showed that multiple metal ions were able to scavenged fluorescence of NCDs (Fig. 2c), whereas the specific quenching effect in the CTAB@NCDs system was achieved only by Hg2+ (Fig. 2d). The above phenomenon was speculated that due to the aggregation and electrostatic interaction properties of CTAB alkyl chain organic molecules resulted in the cross-linking between CTAB with NCDs forming spatial structure, and the sensitization of its fluorescence behavior made Hg2+ be trapped by this spatial structure and then induced fluorescence scavenging [38].

In order to confirm that the introduction of Hg2+ caused a drastic scavenging role at 553 nm (Fig. 2e) was due to the electron or energy transfer between them. A strong chelating agent(l-cysteine)of Hg2+ was added to the CTAB@NCDs/Hg2+ system [39]. As shown in Fig. S3b, the removal of Hg2+ from the CTAB@NCDs surface was achieved using combining the Hg2+ with CTAB@NCDs to form an Hg-S bond, and the fluorescence was restored [40]. In addition, the steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer was measured the fluorescence lifetime of the CTAB@NCDs sample in the presence or absence of Hg2+ (see in Fig. 2b) for decay curve and Table S2 for double index fitting comparison data). Based on the best fitting data, the lifetime decay, from 3.44 ns (PL average lifetime of CTAB@NCDs) to 3.07 ns (PL average lifetime of CTAB@NCDs/Hg2+), indicated that the quenching mechanism was a dynamic process.

The molar ratio of CA and urea, preparation temperature, preparation time, and concentration of NCDs and CTAB affect the fluorescence behavior of CTAB@NCDs. Therefore, the optimization of preparation conditions was carried out first. The results showed that when the molar ratio was nCA: nUrea = 1:6 (Fig. S4a), the preparation time was 8 h (Fig. S4b), the preparation temperature was 180 °C (Fig. S4c), the NCDs concentration was 3.5 mg/mL (Fig. S4d), and the CTAB concentration was 10 mmol/L (Fig. S3a), the best fluorescence performance could be obtained. In addition, the effect of pH on the reaction system was studied (Fig. S5a), and NaAc-HAc buffer with pH 3–8 were selected to determine Hg2+. The results showed that F0/F1-1 decreased with pH, so the NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 3) was applied for the following explorations. Furthermore, experiments towards incubation temperature (15–65 °C) and time (3–40 min) were also performed in this case (as shown in Fig. S5b and Fig. S5c). Eventually, the conditions of Hg2+ detection were selected as follows: incubating for 3 min at 50 °C.

Some metal ions and anions were adopted to evaluate the selectivity of CTAB@NCDs. According to the results indicated in Fig. S6a the F0/F1-1 value obtained by other ions was negligible compared with that of Hg2+, indicating the satisfactory selectivity of CTAB@NCDs for Hg2+ determination. In addition, the interference effects of some metal ions and anions on the reaction system have also been studied. The fluorescence signal of Hg2+ was hardly affected in the coexistence of other substances (Fig. S6b), which showed that the material has good specificity and anti-interference ability to Hg2+, and could meet the requirements of practical application.

The standard addition method was used to detect Hg2+ in lake water and tap water. The water samples came from the campus lake and the tap water of the laboratory of Southwest Jiaotong University. They were centrifuged for 20 min and filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane before the experiment. Then, after adding different concentrations of Hg2+ to the water samples, the content of Hg2+ was obtained according to the standard curve and regression equation. As listed in Table 2 , the recoveries range from 90% to 110%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 10%. Overall, the method showed good accuracy and repeatability in the detection of Hg2+ in actual samples.

In conclusion, we designed a fluorescence quenching probe by hybridizing the NCDs and CTAB through aggregation-induced fluorescence effect and other interactions. The introduction of CTAB has enhanced its QY and selection specificity. Such CTAB@NCDs have been further used for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+. The study provided a new and efficacious Hg2+ sensor in environmental samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to thank for the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21777131 & 22176154) and Science and Technology Department Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 22ZDYF1945). We would like to thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for the experimental conditions.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yayun Ji received her Master's degree at Southwest Jiaotong University in 2021. Her research interests include nanomaterials, adsorption, and chemical sensors.

Xue Zou received her Master's degree at Southwest University in 2014. Now, she is pursuing her PhD degree in Southwest Jiaotong University. Her research interests include functional materials, sensing and degradation of pollutants.

Qiumeng Chen received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry, Southwest University. Currently she is the lecturer in the School of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University. His major interests are environmental analytical chemistry.

Yulu Tian is studying for her M.S. degree in the School of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, China. Her research interests include nanomaterials and chemical sensors.

Zhengjun Gong graduated from Southwest University with a M.S. degree in 2002. Then she received her PhD degree in Sichuan University in 2006. Currently she is the professor and the dean in the School of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University. Her major interests are Environmental pollution control technology and environmental analytical chemistry.

Meikun Fan graduated from Southwest University with a M.S. degree in 2002. Then he received his PhD degree in University of Victoria in 2010. Currently he is the professor in the School of Geosciences and Environmental Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University. His major interests are the chemical process of the environmental surface interface and the development of environmental analytical chemistry methods.
